text: BABWORTH, a parish of Nottinghamshire, 1 m. W of East Retford. Area 5,490 acres. Pop. 577.
BABY. See BABI.

BA'BYLON, a celebrated city of Asia, the capital of the Babylonio-Chaldean empire, the greatest and probably the earliest city of antiquity, having been built, as is generally supposed, on the spot originally occupied by the Babel of Nimrod. Rennell and Rich first succeeded in identifying certain gigantic mounds, and shapeless masses of ruins, which occur on the W bank of the Euphrates, in the vicinity of the modern town of Hillah, in the pashalik of Bagdad, with the remains of a city once "the glory of kingdoms, the beauty of the Chaldees' excellency;" and their reasonings have been assented to by Buckingham, Ainsworth, Fraser, Mignan, and other recent travellers. Hillah is in N lat. 32° 28' 30" [Niebuhr], 32° 31' 18" [Mignan]; 48 m. S of Bagdad, 35 m. SE of the Nahr-Malcha at its junction with the Tigris, the site of the ancient Seleucia, and 130 m. SW of Is, or Hit, on the Euphrates. A brief description of the ancient city, from the pages of Herodotus, Strabo, and Diodorus, is a necessary introduction to our notices of its present state. B., we are informed, stood in the midst of a large plain of deep and fruitful soil. It was divided into two parts by the river Euphrates, which flowed through the city from N to S. The old city was on the E, and the new city, built by Nebuchadnezzar, on the W side of the river. Both these divisions were enclosed by one wall; and the whole formed a complete square 480 stadia in circuit. Each of the four sides of this square had 25 gates of solid brass, at equal distances. The city was composed of 50 streets, each 150 ft. wide, running from the 25 gates on each side, and crossing each other at right angles; besides 4 half-streets, 200 ft. in width, surrounding the whole, and fronting towards the outer wall. It was thus divided into 676 squares, of 42 stadia on each side, along which the houses were built at some distance from each other. These intermediate spaces, as well as the inner parts of the squares, were employed as gardens, pleasure-grounds, and even, according to Quintus Curtius, as arable and pasture-land in a time of siege; so that not above one-half of the immense extent which the walls enclosed was occupied by buildings. The admeasurements now given are those of Herodotus; but Ctesias reduces the circuit of the walls to 360 stadia; and the precise length of the stadium of these authors is, moreover, matter of controversy. Estimating the stadium at 491 ft., and adopting the lowest admeasurements, the area of ancient B. must have been 72 sq. m. But if the stadium be taken at 604.5 ft., and the measurements of Herodotus adopted, the area would exceed 188 sq. m.—The walls were of extraordinary strength, being 75 ft. broad, and 300 ft. high. They were built of bricks, cemented by a kind of glutinous earth called bitumen, which was used in a hot state, and had the quality of soon becoming as hard as stone. It may be doubted, after all, whether these celebrated walls were entirely built of brick. Diodorus Siculus mentions an interior wall of unbaked brick: when rebuilt by Nebuchadnezzar, the original walls are described as having vanished or melted into air. [Quart. Rev. vol. lviii. p. 534.] The walls were surrounded on the outside by an immense ditch, from which the earth had been dug to make the bricks; and which, being always filled with water, added much to the defence of the city. On each side of the river was built a quay, or high wall, of the same thickness with the walls around the city; and gates of brass in these walls, opposite every street, led to the river by means of steps, so that the inhabitants could easily pass in boats from one side of the city to the other. There was also a bridge thrown over the river, near the middle of the city. To prevent any inconvenience from the swellings of the Euphrates, two canals were cut from that river at a considerable distance above the town, which carried off the superabundant waters into the Tigris. From the place where these canals commenced, the sides of the river, both above and below the city, were faced by immense banks to confine the stream still more effectually within its channel, and to prevent all danger of inundation. To facilitate the construction of these works, a lake was dug on the W side of B., about 40 m. in circuit, and 35 ft. deep, into which the river was turned by a canal, till the banks were completed: it was then restored to its former course. This lake continued to receive annually a fresh supply of water from the Euphrates, and was rendered very serviceable, by means of sluices, in watering the lands situated below it. The stream of the river, however, must have found some outlet from such a reservoir.-At the two ends of the bridge over the Euphrates were two magnificent palaces, which had a subterranean communication with each other, by means of a vault or tunnel under the bed of the river. The old palace, on the E side, occupied four of the square divisions of the city, and was surrounded by three separate walls, one within the other, with considerable spaces between them. The new palace, on the opposite side, was about four times as large as the other. The most remarkable structure in the new palace was the hanging-gardens, which Nebuchadnezzar is said to have raised, in order to give his wife Amytis, daughter of Astyages, king of Media, some representation of the mountainous and woody views which abounded in her native country. These gardens occupied a square of 400 ft. on each side; and consisted of large terraces raised, one above the other, till they equalled in height the walls of the city. The ascent from terrace to terrace was by means of steps 10 ft. wide; and the whole pile was sustained by vast arches, built upon other arches, and strengthened on each side by a solid wall 22 ft. in thickness. Within these arches were spacious and splendid apartments, which commanded an extensive and delightful prospect. To form a proper pavement for supporting the soil, and confining the moisture of the garden, large flat stones, 16 ft. in length, and 4 ft. in breadth, were, first of all, laid upon the top of the upper arches; over these was spread a layer of reeds, mixed with bitumen; upon this, two rows of brick closely cemented; and the whole was covered with sheets of lead upon which the earth or mould was laid to a sufficient depth for the largest trees to take firm root. In the upper terrace was a large reservoir, into which water was drawn from the river by some species of engine, to be distributed to any part of the gardens.-Near to the old palace stood the temple of Belus; in the middle of which rose an immense tower, 600 ft. in height, and 600 ft. square at the foundation. This huge pile of building consisted of eight towers, or receding stages, each 75 ft. high, placed one above the other, and gradually decreasing towards the top like a pyramid. The ascent to the summit was accomplished by spiral stairs on the outside, and these, winding eight times round the whole, produced the appearance of as many towers of regularly contracting diameter. In the different stories were lofty apartments, supported by pillars, and used as chapels or temples in the worship of Baal; and on the summit was erected an observatory for astronomical purposes. In this temple, or, as some say, on its summit, was a golden image 40 ft. in height. - Many of the above statements are unquestionably greatly exaggerated; but, after every abatement that can fairly be made, this city is supposed to have formed a regular square, 48 m. in circuit. [Gillies' Hist. of the World, and Rennell's Geog. of Herodotus.] For the space of 26 years after the death of Nebuchaunezzar, B. continued the seat of an imperial court, the station of a numerous garrison, and the scene of a most extensive commerce. It was at length invested about 540 B. C., by the victorious armies of Cyrus the Great. Crowded with troops and furnished with provisions for twenty years, B. long derided the efforts of the besieger, who, on the other hand, left no expedient untried for the reduction of the city. After spending two years before its walls in vain, he was at last presented with an opportunity of effecting his purpose by stratagem. Having learned that a great festival was to be celebrated in the city, and that it was customary with the Babylonians on such an occasion to spend the night in drunkenness and debauchery, he posted a part of his troops at the spot where the Euphrates entered the city, and another at the place whence it flowed out, with orders to march along the channel whenever they should find it fordable. He then detached a third party to open the head of the canal which led to the great lake above described, and, at the same time, to admit the river into trenches which he had drawn around the city. By these means the river was so completely drained by midnight, that his troops easily found their way along its bed; and the gates which shut up the passages from its banks, having been left open in the general riot and disorder, they encountered no obstacle in their progress, but penetrated into the heart of the city, and having reached the outer walls of the palace and found its brazen gates towards the river left open, overpowered the palace guards, slew the king Belshazzar, and received the submission of the whole city within a few hours. From this period B. ceased to be the metropolis of a kingdom; and its grandeur rapidly decayed. Its citizens, however, bore the Persian yoke with impatience. Taking advantage of the disorders in Persia, consequent upon the sudden death of Cambyses, and the massacre of the Magians, they continued, during the space of four years, to make secret preparations for revolt; and at length, in the 5th year of Darius Hystaspis, about 518 B. C., openly raised the standard of rebellion, and drew upon themselves the whole force of the Persian empire. Determined upon a desperate defence, and desirous to make their provisions last as long as possible, they adopted the barbarous resolution of destroying all such persons in the city as could be of no service during the siege, in which they resisted successfully all the strength and stratagems of the Persians, for the space of eighteen months, and only yielded at last to stratagem. On gaining possession of B., Darius ordered its hundred gates, and its impregnable walls, to be demolished; put to death 3,000 of those who had been principally concerned in the revolt; and sent 50,000 women from different parts of his empire to supply the place of those who had been so cruelly destroyed at the commencement of the siege. In the year 478 B. C., Xerxes, the successor of Darius, returning from his inglorious invasion of Greece, passed through B.; and partly from his hatred of the Sabian worship, partly with a view to recruit his treasures, plundered the temple of Belus of its wealth, and laid its lofty tower in ruins. In this state it continued till 324 B. C., when Alexander the Great made an attempt to rebuild this edifice, and to restore its former magnificence; but, though he employed about 10,000 men in the work for the space of two months, his sudden death put an end to the undertaking before the ground was cleared of its rubbish. B. declined rapidly under the successors of Alexander; and was nearly drained of its inhabitants by Seleucus Nicator, who built in its neighbourhood the city of Seleucia or New Babylon. It also suffered greatly from the violence of the Parthian princes; and every succeeding writer bears testimony to its increasing desolation. Diodorus Siculus, B. C. 44, Strabo, B. C. 30, Pliny, A. D. 66, Pausanias, A. D. 150, Maximus Tyrius, and Constantine the Great, as recorded by Eusebius,all concur in describing its ruined condition; and Jerome at length informs us, that, about the end of the 4th cent., its walls were employed by the Persian princes as an enclosure for wild beasts, preserved there for the pleasures of the chase. "Babel," says Ibn Hankel in the 10th cent., "is a small village, but the most ancient spet in all Irak. The whole region is denominated * * - - . . . Z the to the * * . 1 - * Babel from this place." It was visited about the end of the 12th cent, by Benjamin of Tudela, who observed only a few ruins of what he conceived to be Nebuchadnezzar's palace still remaining; but so full of venomous reptiles that it was dangerous to inspect them closely. A similar account is given by other travellers; by Texeira, a Portuguese; by Rauwolf, a German traveller in 1574; by Petrus Vallensis in 1616; by Tavernier, and by Hanway. But so very slight were the vestiges of ancient B., that it was thought impossible to ascertain exactly the spot on which it stood: so completely had been fulfilled the prediction of Isaiah,-" Babylon, the glory of kingdoms, the beauty of the Chaldees' excellency, shall be as when God overthrew Sodom and Gomorrah. It shall never be inhabited, neither shall it be dwelt in from generation to generation; neither shall the Arabian pitch tent there; neither shall the shepherds make their fold there. But wild beasts of the desert shall lie there, and their houses shall be full of doleful creatures; and owls shall dwell there, and satyrs shall dance there: and the wild beasts of the islands shall cry in their desolate houses, and dragons in their pleasant palaces." The striking accomplishment of scripture-prophecies, in the conquest, decline, and desolation of B. is fully illustrated in Rollin's Ancient History, Newton's Dissertations, and Prideaux's Connections. In more recent times Niebuhr, Beauchamp, and Olivier, successively visited Hillah, and described the surrounding ruins; but it is to Mr. Rich that we owe the first laborious examination of them. Mr. Rich has identified the locality of ancient Babylon with Hillah and the surrounding country on the following grounds. 1st. The whole district has been known from the most ancient times by the name of El-aredh Babel, i. e., 'the Land of Babel.' 2d. The distances given by Herodotus from the bituminous fountains of Is, or Hit, and by Strabo, from Seleucia, correspond with the position thus assigned to B. 3d. The nature of the soil, the enormous masses of ruins with which it is covered, the peculiar composition of these ruins, and the extensive use of brick in them, the extent to which they are scattered over the circumjacent country, and the relative position of the larger masses. "From the accounts of modern travellers," says Mr. Rich, "I had expected to have found on the site of B. more or less than I actually did. Less, because I could have formed no conception of the prodigious extent of the whole ruins, or of the size, solidity, and perfect state of some of the parts of them; and more, because I thought that I should have distinguished some traces, however imperfect, of many of the principal structures of B. I was completely deceived. Instead of a few insulated mounds, I found the whole face of the country covered with vestiges of building, in some places consisting of brick walls surprisingly fresh, in others merely of a vast succession of mounds of rubbish, of such undeterminate figures, variety and extent, as to involve the person who should have formed any theory in inextricable confusion." These ruins commence at the village of Muhawil or Mahowel, and extend 12 m. S all the way to Hillah. At the distance of 6 m. W of Hillah stands a vast mound, which Mr. Rich identifies with the ruins of the Birs Nemroud, or the ancient temple of Belus; if an equal extent to the E of the Euphrates be admitted, the measures of Herodotus are fully justified, who assigns a square of 480 stadia to ancient B., or 144 sq. m., -an enclosed space equal to nine times that occupied by London in the commencement of this century. All this enclosed space is full of mounds, remains of ancient walls, deserted water-courses, and ancient embankments. The principal masses on the E side of the river extend from a point about 2 m. N of Hillah, for a space of 3 m. in the same direction, and are chiefly embraced by a long narrow mound (a a) which, commencing near the Mujelibe (m), takes a wide detour to the E, and terminates at the SE corner of an eminence called the hill of Amran (a). Mr. Buckingham has sugm BARBARA BARBARA BARBARA BARBARA BARBARA BARBARA BARBARA BARBARA BARBARA BARBARA BARBARA BARBARA BARBARA BARBARA gested that this may be the wall which Jerome describes as the wall of the city: for it is much more likely that this wall, which was probably perfect 15 centuries ago, should have been the boundary of the hunting-park of the Parthian kings, than the ancient walls of B. of 44 m. in extent. The river bank, to the SW of the hill or tomb of Amran, is skirted by a ruin extending nearly 800 yards from b to k. From the E angle of the river b. commences another mound similar to a a, but broader and flatter, which is the most southerly of all the ruins, and is continued beyond the village of Jumjuma <math>(I)</math>. The whole area within these mounds is 2 m. 600 yds. from E to W, and 2 m. 1,000 yds. from N to S. There are three great masses of ruins on the eastern side of the Euphrates which particularly arrest attention: the Mujelibe or Mukallibe,—the Kasr, or palace,—and the Amran-hill. The last of these is a mass of ruin 1,400 yds. long on the SW face, 1,100 on the E face, and 850 on the N face, of a triangular figure, and rising in some places to 60 ft. above the plain. This vast mass is composed of earth mixed with fragments of brick, broken pottery, vitrifications, mortar, and bitumen; the foot at every step upon it sinks in the loose dust and rubbish of the mass which has been successively emptied of its bricks for modern buildings.—The Kasr (H) is a vast mound 550 yards N of this, and divided from it by a valley covered with tufts of rank grass, and crossed by a low ridge of ruins. The Kasr is 2,100 yds. in circumf., and rises full 70 ft. above the general level. It appears to have been composed of buildings far superior to all the others on the eastern side, as it is one entire mass of furnace-burnt bricks, whereas those of the others are of sun-dried bricks. This large pile of ruin is supposed both by Rich and Porter to have been the terraced palace of Nebuchadnezzar; but it has been so much cut up into ravines, and burrowed into by parties in search of bricks, that it is impossible to guess at the original plan of the structure. In the excavations made here, fragments of alabaster vessels, fine earthenware, marble, and varnished tiles are found; and Mr. Rich disinterred in one part of it an image of a lion or an elephant, rudely sculptured in dark grey stone, and of colossal dimensions. The subjoined cut represents the E face of the Kasr. On a ridge of its mouldering ruins still flourishes a solitary tree called Atheleh, of a species foreign to Babylonia, but recognised as the Arabian atle, a species of tamarisk. It seems to have been originally of enormous size; but only a part of its trunk, about 5 ft. in circumf., now remains, which still supports its evergreen and wide-spreading branches. It must be at least 1,200 years old, as tradition records its existence as far back as the days of Ali, sonin-law of Mahommed, and it is revered by the Arabs as its shade afforded him shelter and repose when faint with fatigue from the battle of Hillah. Perhaps this tree may be a germ from the terraced gardens of B. which once covered the space where it now grows. The embankment on the river side is separated from the hill of Amran, and the Kasr, by a winding valley or ravine 150 yds, broad, and covered at the bottom with nitrous efflorescence. Towards the river it shows an abrupt and perpendicular face; and at the top have been found urns filled with human bones,- A mile and a half to the N of the Kasr, stands the vast mass of the Mukallibe, or Mujelibe, that is, 'the overturned.' It is of an oblong figure, 200 yds. long on the N side, 219 on the S, 182 on the E, and 136 on the W: the elevation of the SE angle is 141 ft. It is nearly visible all the way from Mahawil. It is worthy of observation that in Pietro della Valle's time, the alt. of this ruin was 200 ft., and the base measured about 2,600,—a circumstance proving the liability of the B. ruins to gradual decrease. In the space of 200 years, this mound has diminished 60 ft. in height, and nearly 500 ft. in circumf. Let us suppose its decrease in each preceding cent. to have been at only half of this rate, and the size of the original building would exceed the account given of it by any ancient author. [Keppel.] This ruin is supposed by Pietro della Valle, and by Rennell, to have been the ancient temple of Belus; but this opinion has been controverted by Rich and Porter. This ruin abounds with fragments of all kinds, as bricks both sun-dried and furnace-baked, pottery, bitumen, pebbles, vitrified scoriæ, bits of glass, and mother-of-pearl. In this mass numbers of wild beasts have made their lairs; and in most of its ravines are multitudes of bats and owls; thus literally exemplifying the truth of the prediction that the houses of B. should be full of "doleful creatures; that owls should dwell there, and satyrs dance there." Except the Birs Nemroud the ruins on the W side of the river do not correspond in magnitude to those on the E side.-The Birs Nemroud is 61 m. SW of Hillah, and 9 m. SE of the Mujelibe. It is called by the modern Jews 'the prison of Nebuchadnezzar.' This mass is surrounded by a ruined wall 2,286 ft. in circumf. It stands by itself, distinct from all the other ruins, in the centre of a now solitary waste like the awful figure of Prophecy herself, pointing out the complete fulfilment of her solemn denunciation. The height of the mass on the W side is 198 ft. It is crowned by a solid mass of brick-work rising 37 ft. higher still, and 28 ft. broad. The whole mass is of the finest brick-work, presenting the appearance of an angle of a structure originally quadrangular. It is rent half-way to the bottom; whether by the hand of man, or visitation of the elements, Porter cannot determine, but thinks it to have been effected by lightning. The other parts of the summit are occupied by immense fragments of brick-work, tumbled together, and converted into solid vitrified masses. The subjoined view of the Birs Nemroud, as seen from NNW, is from Captain Mignan's interesting work. A large triangular mound, equal in height to the Kasr, and 1,242 ft. broad, by 1,935 ft. long, stands 270 ft. to the E of the eastern face of the Birs. To the NW of the village of Anana (0) stands another mound 300 yds. long, by 14 ft. high; and 2 m. farther NW is an assemblage of mounds, the most considerable of which is 35 ft. high, extending 3 m. in length. There, in Ker Porter's opinion, are the ruins of the lesser and older palace of the Babylonish kings. A mile beyond this, a number of minor mounds appears; and 31 m. first S, and then SE, towards the Birs, is another vast space covered with ruins extending 13 m. in length. All the mounds now mentioned appear to have been within the circuit of the ancient B., and demonstrate its amazing magnitude. They are all, without exception, of brick; no stone exists in the alluvial soil of the Babylonian delta.—Other large mounds have been explored in the immediate vicinity, as those of Al-Heimar and others, E of Hillah,-the mound of Akerkouf 10 m. NW of Bagdad,—the mounds of Bursa, 4 leagues below Hillah,—besides two other large mounds 3 leagues S of the same place. Indeed the whole tract from Bagdad SW to Hillah, and from Hillah SE to the marsh of Lemlum, is full of ruins, attesting former population and opulence; but all now a dreary waste. Mr. Buckingham in some of the straight lines of mounds in this quarter thought he beheld the remains of the rectilinear streets of B., but Mr. Fraser inclines to regard them as marking the lines of the ancient canals whose banks generally rise above the surface of the ground over which they were led. "The chief objection to the descriptions given of B. by the ancient writers, has been made to its vast size, and to the difficulty of supporting its enormous population, in a country, one part of which (Arabia) was far from fertile, and in an inland situation, difficult of access to distant countries, and with very imperfect means of obtaining their production. To take the latter part of the objection first; -we are perfectly willing to admit that a maritime capital is capable of far greater extension in proportion to the whole country, than a metropolis which is far removed from the coast, or is deprived of the benefit of water carriage; but it must be remembered that Babylon, although not a sea-port, was by no means destitute of this advantage. The productions of Mesopotamia, and a great part of Persia, might be conveyed by the Tigris, and thence into the Euphrates by the Nahr-Malcha, and other canals, which were dug at various periods, from the time of Nebuchadnezzar downwards, to connect those rivers at different points above Babylon; while the produce of the countries to the north of the Persian gulf, might pass up the Euphrates, together with the food derived from Babylonia itself, at that time one of the most fertile districts of the East. True it is, that rich country is now a desert. 'The sea is come up upon Babylon; she is covered with the multitude of the waves thereof; her cities are a desolation, a dry land, and a wilderness; a land wherein no man dwelleth, neither doth any son of man pass thereby.' 'The Arabian shall not pitch his tent there. I will make it a possession for the bittern, and pools of water; and I will sweep it with the besom of destruction, saith the Lord of hosts!' But in the days of Babylon's prosperity, the surrounding country abounded with the fruits of the earth; it was productive as a garden, and in the time of Herodotus, was regarded as the richest part of the most fertile district of Asia. So that, taking into account the small quantity of animal food consumed by the inhabitants of southern climates, as compared with those of northern latitudes, we shall find that Babylonia itself might afford vegetable supplies for a population as great as that of Babylon is supposed to have been. In fact, it is impossible in this respect to compare Babylon with London, as some authors have done, and to say,—because it requires so many acres to furnish food for the inhabitants of London, that therefore Babylon, containing so many more, must have wanted a greater extent of cultivated ground than the immediate vicinity afforded. The nature of the food required, and the wants of the consumers, were totally different—the fertility, beyond all comparison, greater in favour of Babylonia. The population, too, instead of being compressed into crowded streets, as in modern cities, was scattered over a space that rather resembled an enclosed district, where each house is a villa, than a closely-built town; and even the area, which, according to Quintus Curtius, was built upon, did not very greatly exceed that upon which BAC London stands, measuring from the end of Whitechapel to Tyburn turnpike, in one direction, and from Pentonville to the southern extremity of Southwark, in the other; and this, too, exclusive of Knightsbridge, Kensington, Bayswater, Kentishtown, and the other suburbs, which might fairly be taken into London, when comparing it with a city built as Babylon was. Then, with respect to the kind of houses-From Herodotus' specifying that there were many of three and four stories high, it is obvious, that by far the greater number were of one or two. No man, describing London or Paris, would say they abounded with houses of three and four stories, when in fact they contain nothing else; besides, in almost all Eastern cities, the houses seldom exceed two stories in height. If the houses of Babylon were generally low, and in the form of courts, it would not only account for the great space of ground required, but also for their speedy decay, as mud and sun-dried bricks, although sufficient for houses of that description, would immediately fall to pieces when uninhabited and exposed to the action of the weather, and the inundations, which were the consequences of the Euphrates overflowing its banks. The same causes would also account for the total disappearance of the walls. Whatever may have been their original height, we know that in Strabo's time they did not exceed 50 cubits, or 75 feet. The great reduction which they underwent from the time of Herodotus, inclines us to believe that the brickwork was not carried to the top, but that the wall was a breastwork of earth, with a casing or retaining wall of brick at its base. When the wall was reduced by Xerxes, the first operation would be to remove the brick facing, and the earth, having then lost its support, would gradually crumble down, till exposure to the rains would in process of time reduce the mound to the level of the desert. Certain it is, that no traces of the wall have been found by any traveller in that country; and equally certain is it, that the walls of Nineveh, 480 stadia in extent, and 100 feet high, according to Diodorus, are now levelled with the ground, and no vestige of them can be discovered. One word upon the number of inhabitants-Major Rennell thinks the authorities carry them beyond 2,000,000, which he thinks incredible. Now, to say nothing of the number of inhabitants of China-70,000,000 in two provinces alone-we learn from the Bible, and from all ancient records, that the countries of the East were formerly very thickly peopled. While it appears, therefore, that 2,000,000 is by no means so vast as to be incredible, we are inclined to believe that the inhabitants of Babylon, in its most populous time, fell short of that number. From what Strabo says of that city, when comparing it with Seleucia, the greater part was in his time a desert, and Seleucia was larger than what remained of Babylon. Now, seventy years after Strabo wrote, Pliny describes Seleucia as a very large city, containing 600,000 inhabitants. But Selencia was at this time rapidly decreasing, from Ctesiphon having become the winter residence of the Parthian kings; and therefore, when it contained 600,000 inhabitants, it was probably considerably less than one-half of Babylon in its original state, which would make the population of Babylon under 2,000,000."-Edin. Rev., vol. xlviii. The principal opponent of the theory which identifies the plain of Shinar with that of Babylon is Dr. Beke, who in his Origines Biblicæ contends that at the period when the tower of Babel was built the plains of B. were actually covered by the waters of the Persian gulf! Dr. Beke founds his opinion on the supposed geological features of the district; but Mr. Ainsworth has furnished proof that while Dr. Beke's Shinar in Upper Mesopotamia agrees in no particular with the description of that district given in the Bible, the alluvial formations of Babylonia did not, at the period when the tower of Babel was built, differ greatly in extent or appearance from their condition at the present day; and that the increase of land, by the deposition of alluvium at the head of the Persian gulf, has certainly not exceeded 30 ft. per annum. Strabo says the Euphrates was a stadium [500 ft.] in breadth at B. Rich found it to be 450 ft., with an average depth of 21 fath., and a current of 2 knots an hour. Mignan, in May 1828, found the Euphrates flowing past Hillah at 7 knots an hour. Mr. Buckingham was struck with the general resemblance of the stream at B. to the Nile above Cairo. Its banks were lined with date-groves, and in the centre were some sand-banks covered with rushes. Authorities.] Rich's First and Second Memoirs on the Ruins of B., 1815 and 1818.—Sir R. K. Porter's Travels, Lond., 1822.—Mignan's Travels in Chaldwa. Lond., 1829.—Buckingham's Travels in Mesopotamia.—Ainsworth's Researches in Babylonia, Lond., 1838. -Fraser's Mesopotamia in Cabinet Library. Edin.—Keppel's Personal Narrative. Lond., 1834.—A curious dissertation on the material of the B. structures will be found in the 58th vol. of the Quarterly Review; and the 48th vol. of the Edinburgh Review contains an interesting article on the ruins of B.
BACA (Punta Lengua DE), a headland on the coast of Chili, in S lat. 30° 20' 3", W long. 71° 49'.
BACA. See BAZA.
BACACAY, a town on the W coast of the island of Negros, one of the Philippines.
BACAIM, a town on the island of Salsette, 24 m. N of Bombay.
BACALAN, a town of Great Bukharia, 45 m. W of Anderab.—Also a hamlet of France, in the dep. of Gironde, com. of Bandeaux.
BACALAR, a town of Yucatan, on a stream flowing from a lake of the same name, 36 m. SW of Valladolid.
BACALHA'O, a river of Brazil, in the prov. of Goyaz, which passes Tocantins, and flows into the Bagagem.
BACAMARTE, a village of Brazil, in the prov. of Parahiba, 12 m. from Campina-Grande.—Also a sierra in the same prov., connected with the Cordillera Borborema.
BACANOF, a town of Poland, in the gov. of Cracovia, 6 m. SE of Stobnica.

BACCALLAO, or BACALIEU, an island off the SE coast of Newfoundland, the N point of which is in N lat. 48° 9′, W long. 52° 44′ 46″. It is about 3 m. in length from N to S, and 13 m. broad. The channel between it and the main is 2 m. wide. It is supposed to have been the first land of North America sighted by Cabot, on the 24th of June, 1497.—There is another island of the same name in the bay of Notre Dame, in N lat. 49° 41' 50", W long. 54° 28' 48".

BACCANO, a town in the States-of-the-Church, 21 m. N by W of Rome, on a lake of the same name, which discharges itself into the Tiber. It is situated on the NW side of the crater of an extinct volcano.

BACCARAT, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Meurthe, arrond. of Luneville. - The cant. comprises 30 com.—The com. and v. is on the Meurthe, 27 m. ESE of Nancy. Pop. 2,809. The most extensive flint-glass works in France are established here.

BACCHIGLIO'NE, a river in Venetian Lombardy, which, rising in the Alps, flows in a SE direction past Vicenza, then passes Padua, and enters the Adriatic at Brondolo, opposite Chioggia, after a course of 90 m. It was the Medoacus Minor of the Romans. It is connected by a canal with the Brenta.
BACH, a river of Arctic America.
BACHANT, a commune of France, in the dep. of Nord, cant. of Berlaimont. Pop. 674.

BACHARACH, a town of Prussia, in the prov. of the Lower Rhine, 22 m. SE of Coblentz, on the 1. bank of the Rhine. Pop. 1,650. It is celebrated for its red and white wines. It is encircled by antique walls; and the castle of Stahleck, the ancient seat of the counts palatine, crowns a hill behind the town.
BACHELLERIE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Tarrasson, on the Cerne. Pop. 1,446.
BACHI. See BASHEE.

BACHIAN, one of the Molucca islands, in S lat. 0° 30', E long. 127° 30'. It is 54 m. in length, and 20 m. in breadth, and is separated by a narrow channel from Gilolo. The inhabitants are Malay Mahommedans. Their chief town is called Zabongo.
BACHMUTH, or BAKMUD, a town of Russia, in the gov. of Ekaterinoslav, on a tributary to the Donetz, in N lat. 48° 30'. It conducts a considerable trade in salt and horses.
BACHTA, a river of Siberia, flowing into the Jenisei to the S of Inbatskoi.
BACHTIYARI. See BAKHTIYARI.
BACHU. See BAKU.
BACHY, a town of Guinea, in N lat. 6° 55', 7 m. NE of Jenna.
BACILLY, a commune of France, in the dep. of Manche, cant. of Sartilly. Pop. 1,623.

BACK, a river in co. Baltimore, Maryland, U. S., flowing into the Chesapeake 4 m. N of Patasco.— Also a creek of the Chesapeake, in co. Cecil, into which the Dalaware and Chesapeake canal opens.— Also a river in Arctic America, flowing into the S arm of Coronation gulf, in N lat. 66° 30'. BAC
BACK POINT, a headland of Arctic America, at the extremity of Gwydyr bay, in N lat. 70° 20', W long. 149°.

BACKERGUNGE, a district of Hindostan, in the eastern Sunderbunds, bounded on the E by the Puddah or Great Ganges; on the S by the bay of Bengal; and on the W by the Hooringotta; and comprising a superficies of 4,564 sq. m. This territory, notwithstanding its proximity to the sea, is remarkable for its fertility. Periodically overflowed by the waters of the Ganges, and enriched by their alluvium, it produces annually two rice crops of such abundance as to render it the principal granary of Calcutta both for exportation and consumption. Considerable tracts, however, are still covered with jungle infested with tigers of the most formidable dimensions, and the rivers swarm with alligators. The district has suffered much from inundations, An invasion of the Mughs formed a desolating sequel to one of those calamities which occurred in 1574. Another great inundation took place in 1822, in which 10,000 of the inhabitants lost their lives, and a great amount of property was destroyed. The climate is generally insalubrious. In 1801 the pop. was estimated at 926,723, of whom five-eighths were Hindus, and the majority of the remainder Mahommedans. Some Portuguese colonists, descendants of those who in 1666 were invited by the Nabob Hagta Khan to enter his service, still exist near the coast, but in a deplorable state of degradation. They are remarkable for the darkness of their hue. The Dacoits, a piratical horde by whom the rivers were formerly greatly infested, have of late years been brought under the restraints of legislative authority. - The town of Backergunge, formerly the cap. of the district, is situated on a small inosculation of the Ganges to which it gives its name, 87 m. S of Decca, in N lat. 22° 42′, and E long. 90° 26′. It has considerably declined since 1801, when, in consequence of the separation of the district from Dacca Jelalpur, the courts of judicature and of revenue were removed to Burrishol. B. has extensive commerce in rice, salt, and cotton fabrics.
BACKFORD, a parish of Cheshire, 4 m. N by W of Chester. Area 3,320 acres. Pop. 556.
BACKNANG, a town of Wurtemberg, in the Neckar circle, on the Murr. Pop. 4,100. A celebrated horse-market is annually held here.
BACKOFEN, a town of Bohemia, in the circle of Bunzlau, on the l. bank of the Iser, 6 m. NE of Jung-Bunzlau. Pop. 1,100.
BACKSTAIRS PASSAGE, the channel leading from Encounter bay into the gulf of St. Vincent, between Kangaroo island, and Cape Jervis in S Australia.
BACKWELL, a parish in Somersetshire, 8 m. SW of Bristol. Area 2,750 acres. Pop. 1,161.
BACKWORTH, a township in Earsdon p., Northumberland, 5 m. NW of North Shields. Pop. 413.

BACO, a town on the NW coast of Mindoro, one of the Philippines, the cap. of the island. The environs are well-watered by springs from the adjacent mountains, which are covered by the sarsaparilla tree.
BACON, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Sistan, 80 m. NNE of Zahreng.
BACONGEN, a town on the W coast of Sumatra, in N lat. 2° 55'.

BACONO, a river of South America, in Venezuela. It issues from the mountains in the vicinity of Truxillo, and, after leaving their narrow defiles, serves as a line of demarcation to the provinces of Varinas and Venezuela; thereafter, passing through the plains, where it irrigates some fertile estates of cocoa, indigo, and sugar-cane, it enters the Guanare, which discharges its waters into the Portugueza. sea, in N lat. 11° 12'.
BACONSTHORPE, a parish in Norfolkshire, 4 m. SE of Holt. Area 1,460 acres. Pop. 326.
BACOUBA, a large and straggling village of Asiatic Turkey, on the E bank of the Diala, 35 m. NE of Bagdad.
BACOUNIERE (LA), a commune of France, in the dep. of Mayenne, cant. of Chaillaud. Pop. 1,916.
BACOUPA, a chapelry in Rossendale, in Lancashire, 6 m. N by W of Rochdale. Pop. 1,526.
BACQUEVILLE, a canton and commune of France, in the dep. of Seine-Inferieure, arrond. of Dieppe. The com. and v. are 10 m. SW of Dieppe, on the Vienne. Pop. 2,685. The cant. comprises 25 com.
BACRAS, a town of Sennaar, 20 m. SE of the town of Sennaar, on the route to Gondar.

BACS, or Bats-Bodrogh, a circle, or administrative prov. of Hungary, between the rivers Danube and Theiss. Area 1,250,000 jochs, or 1,785,700 acres, of which nearly one-half is arable, and onethird is in pasturage. The principal stream is the Mosztonga. There are numerous lakes, some of which are saline. The Bacser or Emperor Francis' canal, runs between Monostorszeg on the Danube and Foldvar. Its length is 70 m. Pop. in 1837, 486,755, of whom nearly one-half were Catholics. There are 4 towns in this prov., viz., Zombor, Theresienstadt, Neusatz, and Bacs.
BACS, or Barsch, a town in the above prov., on the Mosztonga, NW of Neusatz. Pop. 2,743.—Also a town in Wallachia, on the road from Slotina to Bukarest.
BACSEVISKA, a town of Servia, on the Drina, 30 m. S of Zwornik.

BACTON, a parish in Herefordshire, 2 m. NW of Abbey-Dore. Area 950 acres. Pop. 140.—Also a p. in Norfolkshire, 41 m. NE of North Walsham. Area 1,730 acres. Pop. 513.—Also a p. in Suffolk, 5 m. N of Stowmarket. Area 2,380 acres. Pop. 800.
BAD-RIVER, a stream in Upper Canada, connecting Rainy lake and the lake of the Cross.
BADA, a village of Albania, on the Oros river, at the base of Mount Ibyla.

BADA'GRY, a port of Guinea, on the bight of Benin, the cap. of a small territory 12 m. from the mouth of the Lagos, on the W branch of that river, in N lat. 6° 20', E long. 3° 20'. Pop. 10,000. The territory is fertile. The natives, whose religion is Mahommedanism, support themselves chiefly by fishing, and the cultivation of the yam and Indian corn. Oranges, limes, cocoa-nuts, plantains, and bananas grow here in luxuriance. Deer, antelopes, and buffaloes, are numerous in the surrounding forests. The Landers, during their residence here in the month of March, found the therm. ranging between 86° and 94°.

BADAJOZ, a prov. of Spain, bounded on the N by Caceres; on the E by Ciudad-Real; on the SE by Cordova, Seville, and Huelva; and on the W by Portugal. It is divided into 15 partidos: viz., Albuquerque, Almendralejo, Badajoz, Castuera, Don Benito, Fregenal de la Sierra, Fuente de Cantos, Herrera del Duque, Jerez de los Caballeron, Llerena, Merida, Olivenza, Puebla de Alcocer, and Villaneuva de la Serena y Zafra. These are subdivided into 175 pueblos. The area of the whole is 596 sq. leagues. Pop. 310,906. The principal rivers are the Tagus and the Guadiana. The soil is fertile, and produces grain, fruit, wine, and oil.

BADAJOZ, an ancient and strong city of Spain, the cap. of Estremadura, and of the above division. Pop. 12,000. It stands upon an eminence, a point of land at the union of the Rivollas (o o) with the BAD

BACON'S ISLE, a small island in the Chinese | Guadiana (k k), on the left side of the latter river, 12 m. E of Elvas, the frontier-fortress of Portugal in this quarter, 82 m. NNW of Seville, and 49 m. S of Alcantara, in N lat. 38° 38'. The eminence which it occupies, gradually sloping upwards from the plain, terminates precipitously at the castle, C, in the subjoined plan. This hill may be regarded as the extremity of the range of the mountains of Toledo; although Fort Christobal (d), a strong fort on the Portuguese side of the river, and completely commanding the town, may more properly be considered as the extreme point of the range. On the other three sides of the city extends a vast plain, once covered with vine and olive plantations. B. is what military engineers denominate a fortress of the third order; being neither so strong by nature, nor so healthy by situation as Elvas; but as a bulwark of Spain, it is very strongly fortified, and protected by two castles. It is entered by five gates; the streets are narrow, and frequently crooked. Unlike other Spanish towns, it is destitute of fountains; but is well-supplied with wells and cisterns. Outside of the gate called Los Palmas, on the NW of the town, on the road to Portugal, is a handsome bridge across the Guadiana, of 28 arches, 1,874 ft. long, and 23 ft. broad, constructed in 1596. On the Campo side, this bridge has a Tête de Pont (e), and in the centre there is a fine fountain. The river beneath is fordable at every part in the summer-months; but in the winter, it entirely fills its wide channel with a deep and rapid flood. A promenade of poplar trees extends along the bank of the Guadiana, quite close to the town. The interior of the town is clean; and the houses are, for the most part, large and commodious. The market-place, near the castle, is a fine square, containing the government offices. The cathedral is a plain and heavy-looking building, but its interior is magnificent and glittering. The bishop of Badajoz, suffragan of the archb. of Santiago, has under his inspection a cathedral-chapter, an archdeaconry, and 53 parishes. B. is the residence of the captaingeneral and intendant of Spanish Estremadura.—The citadel or castle is built on a calcareous rock rising 300 ft. above the level of the Guadiana, on the S side of it, and commanding the town, and the confluence of the two rivers. The town is also protected by 8 bastions with a counterscarp and glacis, and 4 outworks, viz., Fort Christobal (d); St. Roque (g); Fort Picurina (h); and Fort Pardaleras (i). B. has stood several memorable sieges. It was several times taken and retaken by the Moors and Christians. In 1658, it was besieged in vain by the Portuguese; and in 1661 they were totally defeated here by the archduke John of Austria. During the Succession war it was besieged by the allies under the command of the earl of Galway, and the Marquis Das Minas, who were defeated under its walls by the French, commanded by the marechal de Thesse, and compelled to raise the siege. * Kellermann and Victor failed before it in 1808 and 1809. In the following year it was scandalously given up to Soult by a traitor, at the moment that Massena had begun his retreat, and Beresford was hastening with 20,000 men to its relief. The want of the requisite materials for a siege of the place prevented Wellington from attempting its instant recovery; but on the 16th of March, 1812, while Soult and Marmont were too far separated to get up in time for its relief, the British commander-in-chief suddenly pounced upon it, and in twenty days made himself master of it. The defence was conducted with consummate skill by Philippon and a garrison of 5,000 men. The British lost in killed and wounded before this place 3,860 men; the Portuguese, 1,010. General Philippon, with his staff and about 400 men, escaped to Fort Christobal, but surrendered next day. it falls.
BADAKHERA, a town of Hindostan, in the pergunnah of Ashtah, in Malwah, 21 m. from Sehore.

BADAKSHAN, a khanate of Turkistan, situated between 36° and 38° N lat.; the eastern part of the ancient Bactria. It is one of the most mountainous countries in nature, and seemingly corresponds to the province of Bubacene, conquered by Alexander the Great, which is represented by Curtius as the richest in the world for gold, pearls, and precious stones. It is bounded on the N and NE by the stream of the Upper Oxus; on the E by the Belur; on the SE and S by the Kaffiristan, separating it from Afghanistan and the Cobis of Kashgar; and on the W by Kunduz and Hissar. It is nearly of a triangular figure; the base being the course of the Oxus, the two sides the Belur-Tagh, the upland of Huzrutimam, and the mountains of Talikshan; whilst the apex is the narrow angle which connects the Belur and the Hindukush. Its length, from E to W, is 250 m.; and its greatest breadth upwards of 150 m. from S to N. Strictly speaking, however, its northern boundary is not the Oxus, but the lofty range of mountains which bound the valley of the Upper Oxus, from its remotest source, on the N and NW, to its confluence with the Kokcha at Kajaghar, for more than 300 m. Badakshan seems to be the generic name for the whole tract enclosed in the upper basin of the Oxus. It is composed of two great divisions, the valley of the Oxus, and that of the Kokcha; or Western and Eastern Badakshan. The latter river,—sometimes called the Badakshan,—rises in the Belur-Tagh. It unites with the Penj or Hammu; and the confluent stream forms the Oxus.—The scenery of B. is very beautiful. Its mineral productions are iron, salt, sulphur, and lapis lazuli. The celebrated ruby-mines of B. are still wrought. Though Badakshan be commonly ranked as one of the Uzbek states, and included in Turkistan, yet it has always maintained its independence, and its inhabitants are of a different race, and speak a different language from the Turkish. They are Tajiks, and denominated Badakshis; but towards the W there are many camps of wandering Uzbeks. The religion of the natives is the Mahommedan, which they probably received from the Arabs who became their neighbours after the conquest of Persia and Turkistan. They are governed by a sultan, whose revenue is stated to be 6 lacks of rupees, or £75,000, and his military force is estimated at 10,000 matchlock-men.—Elphinstone.—Burnes.—Fraser.
BADALONA, a town of Spain, in Catalonia, 8 m. NE of Barcelona, near the coast. Pop. 4,875.
BADAMY, or BADAUMY, a pergunnah and fort of Hindostan, in the prov. of Bejapur, 55 m. NE of Darwar, in N lat. 15° 55'. Pop. 2,500. This fort was taken by the British forces in 1818.
BADANA, or BADRABINAR, a town of Servia, in the sanjak of Semendria, on an affluent of the Drina, 40 m. SW of Belgrade. BAD
BADARWAL, a town in the Kohistan of Lahore, 40 m. SSE of Cashmere.
BADBURY, a hundred in the Wimborne division of Dorsetshire, comprising 9 parishes. Area 30,550 acres. Pop. in 1831, 5,967; in 1841, 6,363.
BADBY, a parish in Northamptonshire, 2 m. SSW of Daventry. Area 2,370 acres. Pop. 624.

BADDAS, or Vaddas, a race of Singhalese, who live in the inaccessible mountains and forests of Bintane, behind Batticolo, in the island of Ceylon. They are a robust, hardy race, who appear to have maintained their independence of the sovereign of Kandy, and cautiously abstain from all connexion with the surrounding pop. except in bartering ivory, deer-skins, dried flesh, and honey, for salt, arrows, and cloth.
BADDELY, a river of Lower Canada, in Saguenay co. It is supposed to connect Lake Kiguagomi with Lake Kiguagomishish, into the latter of which
BADDESLEY (NORTH), a parish in Hampshire, 35 m. NE of Romsey. Area 2,570 acres. Pop. 302.
BADDESLEY-CLINTON, a parish in Warwickshire, 12 m. SE of Birmingham. Area 1,140 acres. Pop. 115.
BADDESLEY-ENSOR, a parish in Warwickshire, 3 m. WNW of Atherstone. Area 1,340 acres. Pop. 579.
BADDILEY, a parish in Cheshire, 3 m. WSW of Nantwich. Area 2,080 acres. Pop. 275.
BADDINGTON, a township in the p. of Acton, Cheshire, 2 m. SSW of Nantwich. Pop. 137.
BADDOW (GREAT), a parish in Essex, 2 m. SE of Chelmsford. Area 4,030 acres. Pop. 2,022.
BADDOW (LITTLE), a parish in Essex, 2 m. N of Danbury. Area 1,830 acres. Pop., with the hamlet of Middle Mead, in 1841, 592, of whom 175 were in the hamlet.
BADDRA, a village in Beluchistan, 20 m. N of Dadur. Pop. 500, chiefly Afghans.
BADE DU FORMOSA, a town of Upper Guinea, on the N arm of the Quorra, not far from its mouth.
BADEBORN, a town of Prussia, in the duchy of Anhalt, 5 m. from Ballenstadt. Pop. 1,100.
BADEFOLS D'ANS, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Dordogne, cant. of Hautefort. Pop. 1,157. There are iron-works here.

BADEN, a German grand-duchy, created in 1805 out of the margraviate of Baden-Baden. It is situated in the south of Germany, between 47° 32' and 49° 47' N lat.; and is bounded on the N by the grand-duchy of Hesse; on the NE by Bavaria; on the E by Wurtemberg, and the principality of Hohenzollern-Sigmaringen; on the SE by the Zeller-see, the western arm of the lake of Constanz; on the S by Switzerland; on the W by France; and on the NW by Bavaria. It is a compact territory, with the exception of the burgh of Schlüchten, which is enclosed in Wurtemberg; and, on the other hand, it encloses part of the canton of Schaffhausen, and the fortress of Hohentwiel belonging to Wurtemberg, in its boundaries. It extends from the outlet of the Rhine from Lake Constanz, to the confluence of the Neckar. Its length from N to S is about 150 m.; its greatest breadth, 100 m.; but it is very narrow towards the centre. Its superficial extent is reckoned by Stein at 6,000 sq. m.; and its pop. at 1,090,910. Hassel states the territorial extent at 5,859 sq. m., and the pop. at 1,001,630. This calculation, however, was made in 1817. An official account states the pop. to have been 1,294,131, in 1840; 1,335,200, in 1843; and 1,349,930, in 1846. Physical features.] Baden is chiefly a hilly country, but it has several pleasant valleys, and one noble plain stretching along the right bank of the Rhine. The declination of the surface is mostly towards the Rhine, to the basin of which belong nearly all the rivers of the country. The soil is on the whole very productive, especially in the valley of the Rhine, and along the Neckar; along the Rhine we find a mild delightful climate, and a luxurious vegetation. From Mannheim down to Basle, all the plants of the south of Germany maintain their full beauty; even almonds and chestnuts are raised in this district. In the elevated regions of the Black-forest, and the Odenwald, there is a long winter, and scarcely any sected throughout by deep picturesque valleys. Its length is about 83 m.; its greatest breadth 37 m. The highest points are the Feldberg, 4,650 ft., and the Belchen, 4,397 ft. in alt. Several remarkable stalactite caves occur in this chain. To the N of the Neckar is the Odenwald, - a continuation of the Black-forest, of inferior height, and chiefly composed of sandstone. This range is intersected by a number of well-watered valleys, and is densely covered with oaks, beeches, and pines. Its highest points are the Katzenbückel, 2,180 ft., and the Winterhauch, 1,752 ft. above the level of the sea. Its SW point, the Heiligenberg, in the vicinity of Heidelberg, is 1,148 ft. in height. The Kaiserstuhl, between the Rhine and Treisam, is an independent volcanic group. Its highest point has an elevation of 1,763 ft.—A range of steep hills runs along the W and N shores of the lake of Constanz. Rivers.] The Danube has its sources in this country, at Donau-Eschingen, but runs through only a small part of it, and is here a mere mountainstream. Of more importance to Baden is the Rhine, which forms its southern boundary; to its basin belong the Mayne, the Neckar, the Kinzig, the Murg, the Wutach, the Pfinz, the Wiesen, and the Elz.— Among the lakes, that of Constanz is by far the largest. It lies 1,161 ft. above the sea; but has not been entirely frozen since 1695. It is said to contain 26 species of fish, and 20 of shellfish. That arm of it called the Zeller-see, or Untersee, belongs entirely to B. Its navigation is animated, but is not much extended to the general commerce of Germany. Among the smaller lakes are the two Ilmensees, the Schluchsee, the Feldsee, 2,440 ft. above the sea, the Eichenersee, and the Mummelsee. Climate.] The mean temp. of the grand-duchy is estimated at 50° 45' Fahr. In the Black-forest it is considerably lower; and here the transition from winter to spring is often very abrupt. On the Danube and the lake of Constanz, it is 52°. In the valley of the Rhine the following mean temp. has been observed: in N lat. 48° 04', Mannheim, The mean fall of rain at Friburg was 28 inches per annum. Soil.] B. is one of the most fertile territories of Germany, and exports annually a large quantity of grain to Switzerland. A good routine of husbandry is here followed; the rearing of cattle is extensively pursued in the regions of the Black-forest; and the forests are likewise in excellent condition. Tobacco, madder, and hemp are extensively grown. Spelt is the grain chiefly cultivated. The total grain produce in 1838 was 1,728,965 quarters. The best descriptions of wine are those of the Ortenau, and the valleys of the Maine and the Neckar. The following return of the extent of land in this state which has been brought under cultivation, or rendered otherwise productive, is from Hörschelmann's edition of Stein's Manual, which assigns the date of the 29th of February, 1832, to it: Morgens. Acres. Arable land, . . 1,363,167 or 1,409,514 Woods and forests, Minerals.] From the sands of the Rhine gold to a small amount is washed. Native silver is found in the mine of Winceslaus near Altowlsach. The mines of Oberwert and Kandern produce excellent iron. Freestone and marble are abundant; and 280,000 cwt. of salt are annually obtained from the works at Durrheim in the Black-forest.-The territory of B. is celebrated for its mineral waters and hot springs. In the capital itself there are upwards of 300 hot springs, all of them impregnated with alum, salt, or sulphur. In one of these baths called 'the kettle,' the water boils furiously, and smokes as if placed over a furnace. A number of hot springs also-occur at Badenweiler, but the temperature is not so great as that of the Baden baths. Manufactures. B. is more an agricultural than a manufacturing territory. Manufactures are chiefly confined to Mannheim, Pforzheim, and Carlsruhe. There are iron-works belonging to government at Albbrugg, which produce about 1,000 tons of iron annually. At St. Blasein there is a manufactory of arms, nails, and hardware articles. Alum and vitriol are manufactured at Schriesheim, Gerspach, and Au. About 10,000 hands are employed in the making of coarse linen; and about 1,200 in woollen manufactures. The inhabitants of the Black-forest have long been celebrated for their dexterity in the manufacture of wooden-clocks and toys. Paper, tobacco, potash, glass, and earthenware form items in the products of industry. B. is a country of extensive transit. Its imports exceed £1,000,000 per annum. As a member of the Zollverein, B. is entitled to a proportion of 4.91 per cent. of the joint-revenue.—A railway, or series of connected railways, intersects the whole country from Basle on the S, proceeding by Friburg, Ettenheim, and Offenberg, to Appenwier, where a branch runs off to Kehl opposite Strasburg; from Appenwier the main line proceeds by Achern, to Rastadt; thence to Carlsruhe, Bruchsal, and Heidelberg, where a branch goes off to Mannheim; it then passes Weinheim, and runs to Darmstadt and Frankfort. This railway system is linked to that on the opposite or l. Basle to Lutterbach and thence up the valley of the Ill, on the I. bank, to Strasburg. Population.] The pop. of B. is of the High German race. Their language is a harsh High German dialect. The three Christian confessions, Catholic, Lutheran, and Reformed, have equal toleration in B., there being no established religion; but the government exercises considerable jurisdiction in the affairs of each establishment. The Catholics form the majority, amounting to 897,040 in 1846. The Lutherans amount to about 248,500; and the Reformed church numbers nearly 100,000 adherents. The Jews number 23,000; and the Mennonites, 1,500 Education. Much has been done for education in this state. There are two universities, -one Catholic, and one Protestant,—of which the latter at Heidelberg, is one of the best in Germany. The former, which is established at Friburg, numbered 301 students in 1840-1; the latter, 654. In general the members of each religious persuasion have their own public schools; but where means are wanting, the school of one confession joins that of another. Sunday schools and schools-of-industry exist in most of the towns and villages; and some of them contain schools-of-art and establishments for acquiring mechanical science. The proportion of children attending schools to the pop., in 1830, was 1 in 6, being the same as in Prussia. All affairs of education are superintended by the 'Board of General studies.' Numerous literary and scientific institutions exist throughout the grand-duchy. In 1847, there were five newspapers for general circulation published in this state, besides several local journals; and the most violent democratic organs of Germany were amongst these. Government.] B. is an hereditary constitutional principality, with the succession in the male line. The legislature consists of two houses. The upper house has 36 members, of whom 8 are chosen by the grand-duke, and one by each university; the principal nobility, the Catholic archbishops of Friburg, and a Protestant prelate, form the remaining members of the upper house. The lower house consists of 64 representatives, who are elected by all males who have attained their twenty-fifth year. They are chosen for eight years, in such rotation that one-fourth of the members of the lower house is renewed every second year. The grand-duke has the exclusive right of proposing laws; but no taxes can be levied without the consent of the legislature. Every circle has its own provincial government, and is subdivided into districts, each having its local magistracy. The Aulic council at Mannheim is the supreme tribunal.—The army amounts to between 8,000 and 10,000 men, exclusive of the militia; but if the whole of the latter were summoned it might be raised to 100,000 men. The contingent is 10,000 men. Revenue. The revenue in 1826 was 20,353,000 francs, or £848,041 10s. 2d. In 1832, it amounted to £1,051,800; and the expenditure to £1,014,040. The public debt in 1810 was estimated at 18,000,000 of florins, or £2,250,000. In 1832 it was stated to amount to £2,210,650; and in 1844 to 27,271,434 Rhenish florins. Circles and towns.] Baden is divided into six circles, which are again subdivided into bailiwicks. There are 108 towns, 36 boroughs, and 2,427 villages and hamlets in this country. In the year 1832, a new political distribution of this state into four circles was made, viz .:- Area in sq. m. Pop. in 1843. of the Lower Rhine, Carlsruhe, the chief town of the duchy, and one of the finest towns in Germany, belongs to the circle of the Central Rhine; and the celebrated watering-place of Baden, which is situated in 48° 46' N lat., and 8° 15' E long., about 18 m. SW of Carlsruhe. Rastadt, on the Murg, in this circle, has been recently fortified. Kehl, also a small town in this circle, on an island formed by the Rhine, opposite to Strasburg, was formerly a strong fortress; but the works were demolished in 1815. Friburg is the chief town of the Upper Rhine circle, and the seat of the Catholic university. Breisach, which had formerly a very strong citadel, also belongs to this circle. In the circle of | ing season; for this town likewise is celebrated for its termanene approximate are tannina ina ina ancient and electric electric electric electric electric electric electric electric electric electric electric electric electric electric electric electric electric electric electr the Lake, which has its name from its situation on the lake of Constanz, lie Hohentwiel, belonging to Wurtemberg, and part of the cantons of Zurich and Schaffhausen. The town of Constanz is on the Rhine, where it flows out of the lake. Mannheim' the chief town in the circle of the Lower Rhine, is beautifully situated on the Rhine, here about 1,200 ft. broad, and over which is a bridge of boats. Heidelberg, on the Neckar, over which there is a bridge 702 ft., in the same circle, is the seat of the Protestant university, founded in 1386, and the oldest of all existing German universities. History.] Within the districts of this duchy-which during the Middle ages belonged to Eastern Franconia—a branch of the Alemannian league had spread itself at a very early period; in the 11th cent, we have the first authentic historical notice of the founder of the reigning house, in the person of Berthold, Count of Breisgau, who built the castle of Zähringen, and assumed the title of duke with the consent of the emperor Henry III. His grandson, Hermann II., who died in 1130, took the title of margrave of Baden. After repeated divisions and reunions of territory, two lines were formed, in the 16th cent., by the two sons of the margrave Christopher L, who died in 1527: Bernhard founding the house of Baden-Baden; and Ernest that of Baden-Durlach. Both were originally Protestants; but the former embraced Catholicism towards the end of the 16th cent. The line of Baden- Durlach, on the extinction of that of Baden-Baden, in 1771, acquired possession of the whole margraviate, then a territory of 1,620 sq. m. In the peace of Luneville, the margrave Charles Frederic obtained a considerable accession of territory and population, and the title of an elector of the empire. On the dissolution of the German empire, and his joining the Rhenish confederacy, he took the title of grand-duke, and obtained the greater part of the territories of the prince of Furstenberg, and the landgraviate of Clattgau, besides the principality of Leinengen: the Breisgau had been previously regained by the treaty of Presburg. The principality of Hohenzollern-Sigmaringen, by treaty of 12th October, 1835, forms an integral part of Baden. Charles Frederic died in 1811, generally and justly regretted. He was succeeded by his grandson, Charles, who married an adopted daughter of Napoleon, the princess Stephania. After the battle of Leipzig, he joined the German confederation. He owed the integrity of his dominions to the support which he received from the emperor Alexander, in the congress of Vienna. A few months before his death, in 1818, a new constitution was introduced; but he did not live to open the chambers. He left no male descendants, and was succeeded by his uncle Lewis, who opened the chambers for the first time on the 22d of April, 1819, but dissolved them on the 28th of July, before they had decided on the budget. In all the following assemblies, misunderstandings seem to have arisen between the states and the government; and the influence of Austria has been disastrously felt of late years in this small state. Lewis was succeeded, in 1830, by his half-brother Leopold.

BADEN, or BADEN-BADEN, a town in the grandduchy of Baden, in the circle of the Middle Rhine, 6 m. S of Rastadt, 24 m. NE of Strasburg, 18 m. SW of Carlsruhe, in N lat. 48° 46'. It stands in a valley surrounded on all sides by wood-covered hills, and watered by the Oes or Oelbach. The narrowness of the valley has compelled the buildings to creep gradually up the slope of a hill. The resident pop. is about 4,500; but during the watering-season, in the months of July and August, is increased to 15,000 or 20,000, the hot-baths of B. being the most celebrated in Northern Germany. The hot-springs-13, or, according to others, 17, in number-burst out of the rocks at the foot of the Schneckengarten, the hill on which the castle stands; and are conveyed by pipes from 115° to 153°; and are said to discharge above 11,420,000 gallons per ann. They are impregnated with salt, alum, and sulphur. "The water is perfectly clear, has a faint animal smell, and a taste somewhat saltish." [Granville.] These baths were knowns to the Romans; and B. was the Aurelia Aquensis of early times, and the cap. for six centuries of the margraviate of B.

BADEN, a town of Lower Austria, 14 m. SSW of Vienna, on the line of the Raab railroad, at the foot of the Styrian Alps, on the Schwächat, an affluent of the Danube. The resident pop. is about 2,600; but, like the above, is greatly increased during the waterwarm baths. The springs, 11 in number, issue from beneath a low limestone rock. The hottest has a temp. of 99°; the lowest temp. is 86°. They yield about 80,640 cubic feet in 24 hours, and supply 16 baths, each capable of accommodating from 50 to 100 persons, for here all bathe en masse. The water is sulphurous, and strongly impregnated with carbonic acid gas. These baths likewise were known to the Romans as the Thermæ Cetiæ or Austriacæ.

BADEN, or OBER-BADEN, a town of Switzerland, in the cant. of Aargau, on the l. bank of the Limmat, 13 m. NW of Zurich, on the high road from Bern to Zurich. It is surrounded by a range of wooded mountains. The resident pop. is about 2,000; but, like its namesakes above noticed, it possesses warm baths, which are much resorted to from June to September, by the inhabitants of Basle, Zurich, and other places in the N of Switzerland. The springs are situated near the Limmat, about \( \frac{1}{4} \) m. below the town. The temp. of the hottest is 107°. The water is clear, and has a sulphurous smell. These baths were the Thermæ Helveticæ of the Romans; and throughout the Middle ages were much frequented.—The town was in early times the cap. of the county of the same name, and the stronghold of the Austrian princes, whose ancient castle of Hapsburg here stands in ruins at the foot of the Wulpelsberg, and from whom, with its domains, it was wrested in the beginning of the 15th cent., by the hardy Swiss. From that period it was held as a bailiwick belonging in common to the seven cantons. Under the French regime B. was incorporated with the canton of Aargau.
BADEN, a commune and village of France, in the dep. of Morbihan, cant. of Vannes. Pop 2,742.
BADEN, or LEUKER-BADEN, a village of Switzerland, in the cant. of Valais, on the Dala, at the foot of the Gemmi, 7 m. N of Loueche. Pop. 474. There are hot springs here also.
BADENOCH, a wild mountainous district in the SE of Inverness-shire, in which the Spey has its sources.

BADENWEILER, a village in the grand-duchy of Baden, in the Middle Rhine circle, 16 m. SW of Friburg, at the base of the Blauen, 1,425 ft. above sea-level. Here are alkaline thermal springs and baths, which are much frequented, and which were celebrated in Roman times. Considerable remains of the baths, dedicated to Diana Abnoba, still exist. In the environs are silver-mines. Pop. 311.
BADESCURE, a peak in the Pyrenees, at the head of the valley of Bun, alt. 10,325 ft.
BADEVAL, a commune of France, in the dep. of Doubs, cant. of Audencourt, 7 m. E of Montbeliard. It has some clock-manufactures. Pop. 363.
BADGASTEIN, a small village of Austria, in the circle of Salzburg, 3 m. from Hofgastein, celebrated for its baths.
BADGER, a parish in Salop, 6 m. NE of Bridgenorth. Area 980 acres. Pop. 137.
BADGINGTON, a parish in Gloucestershire, 3\frac{1}{2} m. NW of Cirencester. Area 990 acres. Pop. 172.

BADGWORTH, a parish in Gloucestershire, 4 m. SW of Cheltenham. Area 1,730 acres. Pop. 903, of whom 210 were in the hamlet of B. in 1841.—Also a p. in Somersetshire, 4 m. SW of Uxbridge. Area 1,470 acres. Pop. 321.

BADIA, a district and town of Venetian Lombardy, in the deleg. and 15 m. WNW of Rovigo, on the r. bank of the Adige. The river is here crossed by a handsome stone bridge, and the town is generally well-built. Two annual fairs are held here; and the place has a considerable commerce in grain, forage, silk, leather, faggots, iron, &c. Its chief manufactures are varnish and china. Pop. 4,000.
BADIA-CALAVENA, a district and town of Ve- BAD netian Lombardy, in the deleg. and 12 m. NE of Verona. Pop. 2,000. The district contains quarries of fine marble.

BADIA-SAN-SALVATORE, a town of Tuscany, in the district of Sienna, on the S side of Monte Amiata, at an elevation of 2,772 ft. above sea-level. An abbey which existed here, but was suppressed in 1782, was the richest and most ancient in Tuscany. Pop. 2,877.
BADIA-TEDALDA, a town of Tuscany, in the circle and 30 m. NNE of Arezzo, on the declivity of the Apennines. Pop. 420.
BADIA-TORRITA, a village of Tuscany, in the circle and 4 m. S of Arezzo. A celebrated Benedictine abbey formerly existed here. Pop. 286.

BADIBU, or BARTIDA, a district of Senegambia, in the Yoloff territory, bounded on the S by the river Gambia, by which it is separated from the territory of the Mandingoes; and on the W by the district of Cower. Its length from N to S is upwards of 75 m., and its greatest breadth from E to W 69 m.
BADIGLIANI, a town of Albania, in the sanjak of Avlona, on the l. bank of the Vojutza or Poro, 5 m. ESE of Premiti, and 16 m. of Aio.
BADINGHAM, a parish in Suffolk, 4 m. NNE of Framlingham. Area 3,390 acres. Pop. 864.
BADJE, a sea-port on the N coast of the island of Sumbawa, in S lat. 8° 15', E long. 118° 15'.

BADJECUNDA, a river on the W coast of Africa, in the Felup territory, flowing into Vintang creek, on the S side of the estuary of the Gambia. It is navigable up to a town bearing its name, 60 m. from Vintain.
BADJOEKIKE, or BADJUKIKE, a large deep bay in the Macassar coast of the island of Celebes, affording excellent anchorage. There is a town at the head of the bay, 17 m. N of Tanete.
BADKHANI, a town of Persia, in the prov. of Khorassan, and district of Comis, remarkable for a fountain, from which, it is said, gusts of wind of great impetuosity have been known to issue.
BADLAND, a town of Silesia, in the circle of Oppeln, near Landsberg. Pop. 800.
BADLAPUR, a town of Hindostan, in the district of Aurungabad, on the r. bank of the Koulans, 35 m. NE of Bombay.
BADLESMERE, a parish in Kent, 4 m. SE of Faversham. Area 820 acres. Pop. 122.
BADLEY, a parish in Suffolk, 2 m. NW of Needham. Area 1,050 acres. Pop. 83.
BADMINGTON (GREAT), a parish in Gloucestershire, 5 m. E by N of Chipping-Sodbury. Area 1,450 acres. Pop. 552.
BADOISKOI, a village of Russia, in the gov. of Olonetz, 39 m. SE of Vitegra, on the Kofja.
BADOLATO, a town of Naples, in Calabria Ultra 2da, 20 m. S of Catanzaro. Pop. 3,394.
BADONG, a district on the W coast of the isle of Bali, in the Sunda group. Pop. 20,000. See Ball.
BADONVILLER, a village of France, in the dep. of Meurthe, cant. of Baccarat, on the Blette. Pop. 2,297.
BADONY (Lower), a parish in co. Tyrone, 8 m. NNE of Omagh. Area 47,921 acres. Pop. 7,784. It contains the v. of Gortin.
BADONY (UPPER), a parish in co. Tyrone, 61 m. NE of Newton-Stewart. Area 38,208 acres. Pop. 5.822.

BADRACHILLUM, or B'HADRACHALAM [i.e. 'the Sacred mountain'], a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Gundana, in the district of Bastar, on the Godavery, 72 m. NW of Rajamundry, in N lat. 17° 57'. Tolls are exacted here upon goods in their transit from the interior to the northern Circars; and there is a celebrated pagoda in the vicinity.
BADRAI, a stream and district of Khuzistan, in Persia, divided from the plain of Sirwan by a range of hills.

BADRINATH, or BHRADINATH, a town of Kumaon, on the Alacananda river, in N lat. 30° 43', E long. 79° 39′, at an alt. of 10,294 ft. above sea-level. There are warm baths here; and a richly endowed temple.
BADSDORF, a town of Bohemia, in the co. of Glatz. There are mineral springs here. Pop. 1,200.
BADSEY, a parish in Worcestershire, 2 m. E by S of Evesham. Area 1,770 acres. Pop. 497.
BADSHOT, a tything in the p. of Farnham, Surrey. Pop., with Runfold, 1,410.
BADSWORTH, a parish in the W. R. of Yorkshire, 4 m. S by E of Pontefract. Area 4,320 acres. Pop. 750. It comprises the townships of B., Thorpe- Audlin, and Upton.
BADU, a town in Senegambia, on the Gambia river, in N lat. 13° 15'.—Also a town in Darfur, in N lat. 13° 42'.

BADULLA, a village and fort of Ceylon, in the prov. of Ouva, 35 m. SE of Candy, in N lat 7° 2'; at an alt. of 2,000 ft. above sea-level. It stands on the slope of a steep ascent, and is surrounded by mountains, amongst which that of Kammunakuli has an alt. of 6,700 ft. [Forbes.] The surrounding country is fertile, and well-adapted for the culture of coffee.

BADUR, a river of Beluchistan, which is supposed to fall into the Arabian gulf, in N lat. 24° 5', under the name of the Dusti. Pottinger crossed it in lat. 28° 37', E long. 64° 30', where its channel was 500 yards wide, but destitute of water.
BADWELL-ASH, a parish in Suffolk, 2 m. N of Great Ashfield. Area 2,000 acres. Pop. 458.
BAELEGEM, a commune and village of Belgium, in E Flanders, 10 m. S of Ghent. Pop. 2,882, chiefly cotton and linen weavers.

BAELEN, a commune and town of Belgium, in the prov. of Antwerp, on the Grande-Nethe, 20 m. SE of Turnhout. Pop. 3,039.—Also a commune and village in the prov. of Liege, 6 m. N of Verviers. Pop. 1,916.
BAENA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Cordova, on the Marbella, 22 m. SE of Cordova. Pop. 5,000. There are salt-works in the vicinity. Numerous Roman remains have been found here.
BÆRENKOPF, one of the summits of the Vosges, near the sources of the Moselle. Alt. 3,064 ft.
BÆREN. See CHERRY ISLAND.
BÆRENSTEIN, a town of Saxony, in the circle of Zwickau. Pop. 1,200.
BAERUM, a village of Norway, 6 m. W of Christiania. There are iron-forges here.
BAERWALDE, a town of Prussia, in Brandenburg, 13 m. NNW of Kustrin. Pop. 2,900.—Also a town of Pomerania, 8 m. W of New Stettin. Pop. 1,300.
BAESRODE, a commune of Belgium, in E Flanders, 5 m. E of Termonde. Pop. 2,910.

BAEZA, or BAEÇA, a town of Spain, in Andalusia, in the prov. of Jaen, on a rising ground, 18 m. NE of Jaen. Pop. 10,800. Its streets and squares are handsome and spacious, and one of the latter is adorned with a beautiful fountain. B. was in former times a place of considerable importance, and carried on an extensive commerce. Under the Moors its pop. amounted to 30,000; Laborde says 150,000. It was subsequently a bishop's see, and the seat of a university, founded in 1533. At present it has much declined from its ancient grandeur; but some of the public edifices, particularly the Gothic cathedral, the colleges of the Jesuits, the church of the Franciscans, the chapel of the ancient university, and the public prison are deserving of attention. Its principal ma- BAF nufacture is that of leather. This town marks the site of the ancient Beatia Bacula, where Scipio routed the younger Asdrubal.
BAEZA, a town of Peru, in the prov. of Quixos. It was formerly rich and populous; but has been so wasted by the Indians that it now contains only a few families.
BAFFA, or Bofo, a seaport on the Grain coast of Africa, in N lat. 5° 7', W long. 8° 51'; which carries on a considerable trade in pepper.

BAFFIN'S BAY, a large bay or gulf, in the Arctic regions, lying between 68° and 78° N lat., and 52° and 80° W long.; between the NE shores of America and the W coast of Greenland; in length, from NW to SE, about 950 m.; in width, varying from 100 m. to 350 m. Its surface is estimated at 266,000 sq. m., not including Davis' straits, connecting it with the Atlantic, which would add about 190,000 sq. m. The depth varies so greatly and so suddenly, that within a few miles the lead showed severally 1,050 fath., and 120 fath. The temp. of the bottom-mud is pretty uniformly 29° or 29½°. The influence of the tides is felt only to the extent of 6 or 8 ft. The shores are generally high, presenting abrupt precipitous ice-clad cliffs, rent into deep ravines, and indented with sounds and bays. The geological formation of the coast is granite and gneiss. Limestone occurs, and basalt. The principal island in this sea is Disco, in a bay of the same name, in N lat. 70°, W long. 58°. It is connected by various channels with the gulf of Boothia, Hudson's bay, and the Polar sea. This bay, though entered as early as 1587 by Davis, was only explored by Baffin and Bylot in 1616; and more recently by Captains Ross and Parry the latter of whom passed through Lancaster-sound, which, in an earlier voyage, the former had supposed to be closed. The black whale, the walrus, and the seal are abundant in this region, as well as the birds which generally frequent the Arctic regions. Vegetation is limited to a few willow bushes, lichens, and mosses.
