The strong current running S through Davis' straits led to the supposition that it must originate from connection with an extensive sea to the N; but examination has shown that the current is only felt to a limited extent northwards; that the bay is really a bay though possibly possessing an outlet called Smith's sound at the extreme N; and that the current is a continuation of the great Arctic current, which, beginning in the polar regions on the E side of Greenland, enters Davis' strait in a N direction to about lat. 60°, when it turns S to pass again through the strait, and mingle with the great Gulf-stream. This sea is navigable only from the beginning of June to the end of September.—Beginning at Cape Walsingham in Davis' straits, the extreme S point of the western coast, and following the coast-line as it trends NW to Barrow-strait, we encounter Cape Durham, Cape Searle, Merchant-bay, Cape Kater, Pond's bay, Cape Moore, Cape Bathurst, Possession-bay, and Cape Liverpool. Crossing Sir James Lancaster's sound, at the E entrance of Barrow's strait, to Cape Horsburgh, we find the coast irregular in outline but turning generally N to the extreme head of the bay. On this part of the coast we have Coburg-bay, Cape Leopold, Lady-Ann-bay Cape Caledon, Jones' sound, unexplored, Cape Hard wick, Cape Clarence, and Cape Mount. The extreme N coast, or head of the bay trending round towards the E, is marked by Cape Hurd and Cape Isabella, both on the W side of Smith's sound yet unexplored, and Cape Alexander, and Cape Saumarez, between Smith's sound and Whale - sound. Tracing the western coast from the latter sound, we have Cape Parry, Cape Hoffner, Cape Stair, Wolstenholme sound, and Cape Dudley Diggs. From this latter cape the coast trends W, in Cape York, Prince Regent's bay, Cape Melville, and Melville bay, to Cape Walker; whence its general course to Holsteinberg on the E side of Davis' strait, and nearly opposite to Cape Walsingham, is to the E of S. The principal points on this part of the coast of Greenland are Cape Lewis, Dunira-bay, Red-head, Allison's bay, Wilcox point, Sanderson's Hope island, Store island, Svartehaven, Cape Cranstown, Omenaks-fiord, Waigat-strait, Disco-island, Disco-bay, Giescke's Soe, Norder Stromsfiord, Cunningham's fiord, and Ramel's fiord.
BAFFIN'S ISLES, a group of rocky islets off the W coast of Greenland, in about 74° N lat., and 57° 45' W long.

BAFFO, a seaport on the W coast of the island of Cyprus, in N lat. 34° 47′ 20"; situated on a rocky eminence close to the sea, with gardens everywhere interspersed among the houses. It has a small harbour, now choked up with sand; and is little resorted to by mariners, except in the summer-season, because it is the most dangerous port of the island, and has no shelter from S winds. The Turks have built a strong castle on the point of a rock to the SW. B., though once important, is now reduced to a place of little consequence. Its only manufacture is that of tobacco. It was an ancient episcopal see; but of several Christian churches, only one now remains. Pieces of fine rock-crystal found in the vicinity are sold under the name of Baffo diamonds; amianthus also, perfectly white, and flexible as silk, finer and more delicately fibrous than that of Sicily, Corsica, or Norway, is found in the neighbourhood. [Clarke.] Numerous ruins and antiquities are dispersed in the vicinity of B., among which are the remains of several highly polished blackish grey marble columns, supposed to have been the materials of a temple of Aphroditis. On the plain surrounding the town, towards the interior, there are some isolated masses of rock, which have been excavated into different forms for dwellings. There is also a subterranean church, dedicated to the Seven sleepers.—B. is supposed to have sprung from the remains of the ancient and celebrated city of New Paphos, which was several times destroyed and rebuilt. The inhabitants of Citium were removed hither when that city was demolished by Ptolemy Lagos. Paphos was almost entirely overthrown by an earthquake, but rebuilt by Augustus, the Roman emperor, and called Augusta. The name is a Venetian corruption of that of Paphos. Old Paphos stood farther to the SE.

BAFING, a large river of Africa, the principal tributary of the Senegal, whose sources Caillie places to the W of Timbo, in a chain of very high mountains, which as seen from Cagnola in Futa Jallo, extend further than the eye can reach from NE 4 E to SW, and appeared to be clothed in rich vegetation. Caillie says that near its source, he found it about 100 ft. wide, and 12 or 18 inches deep, running from S to N, in a channel filled with masses of granite, and with a rapid current. Mr. Thomson says it has its source in a large spring, not 7 m. from its junction with the Wankako; and that at half-a-day's journey from its source, he found it in the dry season, on the 4th of June, flowing with a current 20 yards wide, and more than 3 feet deep.—Caillie's Narrative, vol. i.— Geog. Journ. vol. xvi.

BAFRAH, a town of Anatolia, on the r. bank of the Kizil-Irmak, 20 m. W of Samsun, in N lat. 41° 32' 52", E long. 36° 11' 30". Pop. 2,000. There is a good bridge over the river here. The surrounding territory is fertile in rice, lint, and tobacco.

BAG, or BAUG, a town of Hindostan, in the prov. of Malwah, at the confluence of the Girnah and Wagney, on the road from Malwah to Gujerat by the Udipur pass. About 4 m. to the SSE of this town are some cave-temples.
BAGA, a town of Spain, in the prov. of Barcelona, 20 m. NE of Solsona, on the l. bank of the Bascareny.
BAGAEFESKAIA, a town of Russia, in the territory of the Don Cossacks, on the r. bank of Don, 15 m. NE of Stará-Cherkask.

BAGAI, a village in Algiers, 105 m. S by E of Constantinah, on the site of the ancient Bagasis, on the W bank of a lake from which a river, the ancient Abigas, flows in a SW direction. It was a fortified city with a walled suburb in the 12th cent.

BAGANBUN, a promontory on the S coast of co. Wexford, between Tintern-bay on the W, and Bannow-bay on the E, of historic celebrity as the spot where Robert Fitzstephen, then governor of Cardigan castle in Wales, first landed in 1169.

BAGANAH, or Bazunah, a district of Zanguebar, between Lamu and the mouth of the Juba. BAGARMEH, BAGIRMEH, or BEGHARMI, a district of Tropical Africa. It lies to the E of the Bahr-el-Ghazál, or Antelope's river—or rather the wady of that name, as the river appears to have dried up-by which it is separated from Bornu. The native pop. speak a peculiar language, bearing no apparent affinity to that of the neighbouring They profess Mahommedanism, are more tribes. active and industrious than the tribes by whom they are surrounded, and appear to maintain or to have regained a considerable share of independence under a sultan of their own. B. appears to comprehend a very large region of fertile country, bounded on the E by Darfur; on the S by Darkulla; on the W by Bornu; on the NW by the Wady-el-Ghazal; and on the NE by Bergu. The Wady and Bergu indeed seem to be either subdivisions of B., or a continuation of the same physical region northward and eastward. "The inhabitants of Wa-da-i and Bagharmee," says an Arabian author, "are nearly of the same description. Baghar-mee, however, is now desolated. The cause of its ruin was, as they say, the misconduct of her king, who having increased in licentiousness to such a frightful degree as even to marry his own daughter, God Almighty caused Sabun, the Prince of Wa-da-i, to march against him and destroy him, laying waste at the same time all his country, and leaving the houses uninhabited, as a signal chastisement for his impiety. These provinces are bounded on the N by deserts and dry sands, which, in the spring only, are frequented by herdsmen; and on the S, by a great many countries inhabited by various tribes of sudan (blacks), each of whom speak a different language, and among whom Islamism is not much spread." B. must have recovered from this inroad; for it has long maintained a fierce war with Bornu, and Denham relates many anecdotes of the unconquerable intrepidity of the Bagirmawies. The cap. of B., according to Hornemann, is called Mesnah, or as Koenig writes it, Masgnah. At the beginning of the 18th cent. it was Karnah on the Bahr-el-Ghazal. Denham mentions Kernuk as its cap., but does not describe its situation.

BAGA'R, the great western branch of the delta of the Indus, during last cent., but which has had its direct communication with the main stream cut off by an accumulation of sand at the point of divergence, about 50 m. from the sea. About 30 m. from this point, the Bagar itself became ramified into four branches, the Piti, the Pintiyani, the Juwah, and the Richel. These are now filled with salt water, and may be regarded as so many inlets of the sea, or short tidal channels, running into the low flat treeless shore.—Lieut. Carless.
